2. 
TERRITORIAL ACKNOWLEDGEMENT 
Mayor Hurley respectfully acknowledged that the City of Burnaby is located on the 
unceded territories of the xʷməθkʷəy̓ əm (Musqueam), Sḵwx̱ wú7mesh (Squamish), 
səlilwətaɬ (Tsleil-Waututh), and kʷikʷəƛ̓ əm (Kwikwetlem) Peoples. Each Nation has 
distinct histories and distinct traditional territories that fully or partially encompass the 
city. The City encourages you to learn more about the Host Nations whose ancestors 
have occupied and used these lands, including parts of present-day Burnaby, for 
thousands of years. 
 
3. 
ANNOUNCEMENTS 
Beth Davies’ Retirement 
Mayor Hurley acknowledged the retirement of the Chief Librarian, Beth Davies. The 
following were the Mayor's remarks: 
"Today, I’m honoured to take a few moments to recognize and thank an exceptional 
public servant and community leader, Beth Davies, as she marks her retirement after 
nearly a decade of service as Burnaby’s Chief Librarian. 
Since 2016, Beth has led Burnaby Public Library with care, professionalism and a deep 
commitment to public service. She has guided our library system through a period of 
growth, as both our City and our libraries have expanded to better serve Burnaby’s 
diverse, multicultural population. Beth has been a strong champion for libraries as 
welcoming, accessible spaces for everyone – something that we’re extremely proud of 
here at the City. Under her leadership, Burnaby’s libraries have fostered connection, 
learning and belonging within our community. 
On behalf of Council and the City of Burnaby, I want to sincerely thank Beth for her 
years of dedicated service, her care for both staff and patrons, and her lasting 
contributions to our City. We wish her all the very best in this next chapter." 
 
Councillor Dhaliwal Announcement  
The following were Councillor Dhaliwal’s remarks: 
“It is with mixed emotions that I announce I will not be seeking re-election this fall. 
These emotions are mixed because I have truly valued this work. Representing the 
residents of Burnaby—locally, regionally, provincially, and nationally—has been both an 
honour and a privilege. Over the past 23 years, I have been fortunate to build many 
deep, special and meaningful relationships in our community. This experience has 

Open Council Meeting 
Minutes March 10, 2026 
 
 
3 
broadened my understanding of what matters most in life and, I believe, has made me a 
better person. I know I will miss the responsibility and the privilege of serving the people 
of Burnaby. 
At the same time, I look forward to spending more time with my family and finally doing 
the things we have set aside for far too many years. 
To the residents of Burnaby, thank you for placing your trust in me. Being elected by 
one’s fellow citizens is the greatest honour anyone can receive, and I will remain deeply 
grateful for that privilege. 
I also wish to thank my council colleagues—both current and former—for your support, 
guidance, and advice over the years. 
I want to especially acknowledge Councillor Calendino. Pietro, you have stood by me 
through thick and thin. Your willingness to answer the phone at any hour was invaluable 
and a constant source of confidence. 
To the staff, I extend my sincere gratitude for your patience with my questions, queries, 
requests, and occasional rants and careless remarks that I regretted. I admire your 
enthusiasm, dedication and professionalism that allow councillors and mayors to do 
their work effectively, and I thank you for that. 
Mayor Hurley, thank you for your leadership, support and trust over the past eight years. 
And thank you for giving me the space to be me.  
This is not a goodbye; there are still seven months before the election. With a heart full 
of gratitude, I will continue to work diligently for the residents of Burnaby until the final 
day of this term.” 
Members of Council formally acknowledged Councillor Dhaliwal’s dedicated years of 
service to the City of Burnaby and noted that his absence would be felt by all.

Councillor Lee - Point of Personal Privilege 
Councillor Lee rose on a Point of Personal Privilege at 6:24 pm and noted that, at the 
February 24, 2026, Council meeting, he had stated there were approximately 1,000 calls 
regarding the Douglas Road Shelter. The Chair granted the point. 
During that meeting, Council raised concerns regarding the accuracy of the stated calls. 
Staff responded by advising that they had documented six (6) calls within the specified 
timeframe.  
In response to an inquiry from Councillor Lee seeking further clarification, the General 
Manager Community Safety advised that, while the Bylaw Services data was correct at 
six (6) calls, additional calls had also been received by the RCMP (484 calls) and the 
Fire Department (498 calls). These latter figures were not included in the information 
presented at the February 24, 2026, meeting.

File: 16000 20 
COUNCIL REPORT 
 
TO: 
MAYOR & COUNCILLORS 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT  
SUBJECT: 
BHA – 2026-2030 STRATEGIC PLAN  
PURPOSE: 
To seek Council approval of the Burnaby Housing Authority 
Corporation’s (BHA) 2026-2030 Strategic Plan. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the 2026-2030 Strategic Plan of the Burnaby Housing Authority 
Corporation, substantially in the form attached as Attachment 1 to the report 
titled “BHA – 2026-2030 Strategic Plan”, dated March 24, 2026, and as described 
in section 3.1 of the report, be approved. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
Approval of the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation’s (BHA) 2026-2030 Strategic 
Plan to facilitate the development and administration of non-market housing and market 
rental housing by the BHA in Burnaby on behalf of the City aligns with City policies, 
including:  
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022);  
• HOME: Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021);  
• Burnaby Housing Needs Report (2021); 
• Mayor's Task Force on Community Housing Final Report (2019);  
• Burnaby Social Sustainability Strategy (2011);  
• Burnaby Economic Development Strategy (2007); and 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025). 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
On February 11, 2025, Council approved the first partnering agreement with the BHA 
(“PA No. 1”), which included the City provisioning operating funding as assistance to the 
BHA, in the amount of $2,000,000 per year for five years. At the Closed Council meeting 
on April 22, 2025, Council approved the BHA’s draft strategic plan.  Under PA No. 1, the 
BHA is required to present to Council for approval, a five-year final strategic plan, which 
builds on the previously approved draft strategic plan.   
The final Strategic Plan is intended to guide the growth of the BHA’s portfolio and its 
activities for the remaining four years of the term of PA No. 1.  
The purpose of this report is to obtain Council approval of the BHA’s final Strategic Plan. 

Subject: BHA – 2026-2030 Strategic Plan 
Council Meeting March 24, 2026 .................................................. Page 2 
 
Under the terms of PA No. 1, Council approval of the BHA’s final Strategic Plan and the 
City’s receipt of the BHA’s annual financial statements and current year operating 
budget are conditions of the transfer of the second year of operational funding. It is 
anticipated that copies of the BHA’s annual financial statements and current year 
operating budget will be received as Council correspondence for the April 14 Council 
meeting, and a BHA Assistance Request for the second year of Operating Funds will be 
received at a future meeting. 
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1 Strategic Plan 
Under PA No. 1, the BHA is required to present to Council for approval, a five-year 
strategic plan including a business plan for the BHA’s first project which will achieve not 
less than 50 unit starts by December 31, 2026, as well as a high-level plan of additional 
projects to be pursued by the BHA which housing projects will achieve not less than 750 
unit starts by December 31, 2033. 
The BHA has started site preparation on two sites already approved by Council that will 
result in over 387 units, far exceeding the number of housing starts and well in advance 
of the timeline set out in PA No. 1. These two projects consist of two 6-storey rental 
buildings with 203 units at 10th and Newcombe, and 183 rental townhomes at Byrnepark 
and Marine. 
The Strategic Plan builds on this momentum and sets a more ambitious medium-term 
objective of achieving 1,000 total unit starts by 2030.  
The BHA has provided the Strategic Plan attached as Attachment 1, which was 
endorsed by the BHA Board on January 20, 2026. Staff have reviewed the Strategic 
Plan and are satisfied that it meets the requirements of PA No. 1 and is aligned with the 
City’s objectives in creating the BHA. Staff are satisfied that the proposed approach 
described for future projects is appropriate, consistent with the BHA’s mandate, and 
suitable for inclusion at the strategic planning level. 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Information about the BHA is available at www.burnaby.ca/housingauthority, with more 
detailed project information at the BHA’s website at www.burnabyhousingauthority.ca.  
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Upon Council approval of the BHA’s Strategic Plan and subject to the BHA submitting 
an Assistance Request for the Year 2 Operating Funds, accompanied by its 2025 
annual audited financial statements and 2026 operating budget (which are anticipated 
to be received on the April 14 Council agenda), the requirements in section 5.2(b) of PA 
No. 1 for the provision of $2,000,000 of Year 2 annual operating funds will be met.

outlined a phased approach to implement universal metering city-wide, improve equity in 
water billing, enable lead detection and conservation, and modernize metering 
infrastructure.  Following Council direction, Engineering advanced procurement activities 
to secure a qualified proponent to delivery the program through a RFP process.   
 
 

Subject: CA – Supply and Delivery Water Metering Technology 
Open Council Meeting March 24, 2026 ........................................ Page 2 
 
This recommendation seeks Council approval to award the Supply and Delivery of Water 
Metering Technology for the first phase of the program.  The first phase of the program

commence with installation of approximately 5500 water metering units for meter-ready 
properties. The City will then  commence with installation of water meters for  
approximately 500 properties of the existing 7500 homes with a secondary suite.  At this 
time, general statistical data will be collected for City operational reviews only.  Customers

following installation.

the program schedule and budget.  Review by City staff indicates that Neptune

PURPOSE: 
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the 
development of a modular four-plex housing unit as a pilot project. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT a contract award to Echelon Creative Inc. for an estimated cost of 
$2,626,050, including taxes, as outlined in the report titled “CA – Development of 
Modular Housing Four-Plex Unit at 8315 14th Avenue” dated March 24, 2026, be 
approved; and 
THAT final payment will be based on actual quantities of goods delivered and 
unit prices quoted. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This recommendation is to approve a contract award in the amount of $2,626,050, 
including tax to the Echelon Creative Inc. for this development project. 
The project will demonstrate the feasibility and cost effectiveness of standardized modular 
construction for small scale multi-unit housing in low density neighbourhoods. This pilot 
project forms part of a broader initiative supported by Modular BC and the National 
Research Council to advance low carbon construction methods and scalable housing 
solutions.  The anticipated project delivery is approximately 6 – 8 months and typically, 
modular housing has an expected useful life of about 50 to 60 years. With proper 
maintenance and periodic upgrades, the lifespan can extend beyond 80 years. 
The estimated total project cost is approximately $2.63 million, with confirmed external 
funding contributions of $350,000 from FortisBC and National Research Council, resulting 
in a net capital requirement of approximately $2.28 million for the City. 
Echelon Creative Inc. will act as the design-builder and will coordinate procurement, 
construction oversight, and project delivery.  The City will retain ownership of the land and 
final building, which is intended to be leased as part of the City’s residential rental 
portfolio. 
 

Subject: CA- Development of Modular Housing Four-Plex Unit at 8315 14th Avenue 
Open Council Meeting March 24, 2026 ........................................ Page 2 
 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The City’s Procurement Policy requires that contracts for goods, services, and 
construction with values over $500,000 be approved by Council following a competitive 
procurement process. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The City of Burnaby is participating in a modular housing pilot initiative intended to 
evaluate the use of standardized factory-built construction for small-scale, multi-unit 
housing developments. The project site is located at 8315 14th Avenue. 
The pilot project is being undertaken in collaboration with Modular BC and forms part of 
the National Research Council’s Platform to Decarbonize the Construction Sector at 
Scale. 
The proposed building will include: 
• Approximately 5,319 sq ft of gross floor area 
• Four residential units 
• Three storeys 
• One accessible dwelling unit 
• Private outdoor space for each unit 
The pilot will provide data and lessons learned that can inform future modular housing 
developments within Burnaby and across the province. 
Architectural drawings have been prepared and the design generally complies with 
Burnaby zoning requirements. Preliminary geotechnical review indicates the site is 
suitable for conventional foundation systems.

builder and will be responsible for:

Construction oversight;

Risk management; and

Project closeout. 
 
Following a Notice of Intent, Echelon Creative Inc. will enter into an CCDC agreement 
with the City.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
A development proposal enquiry has been received to permit the construction of an 
amended mixed-use high density commercial/residential strata and rental development 
at 3856 and 3870 Carrigan Court, to replace the development concept approved 
through Rezoning Reference #20-18. The amended development proposal is comprised 
of a total of four high-rise buildings on the site with approximately 1,133 strata units, 141 
non-market rental replacement units and 300 non-market inclusionary units. The 
purpose of this report is to provide Council with information on the development 
proposal enquiry and to recommend that staff be authorized to accept a rezoning 
application and to continue to work with the applicant towards a suitable plan of 
development for presentation to Council at a future date.

adopted by Council: 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025), 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022), 
• Economic Development Strategy (2007), 
• Social Sustainability Strategy (2011), 
• Environmental Sustainability Strategy (2016), 
• Transportation Plan (2021), 
• HOME: Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021), and 
• Rental Use Zoning Policy (2020).

respectively in the Official Community Plan (see Attachment 1). The Official 
Community Plan (OCP) requires that the base designation of General Commercial

comprised of 3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858 and 9898

Development Guidelines for the phased mixed-use redevelopment of the City of 
Lougheed (TCOL) site and select properties to the south in line with the adopted 
Lougheed Town Centre Plan. The Master Plan provides direction related to the

the total Inclusionary non-market rental unit requirement of the Rental Use Zoning 
Policy for the entire site.

3.1 The amended development proposal, as shown in Figure 1, for Phase 1 of the 
Austin-Gatineau site is for two strata residential towers of 77 and 87 storeys, 
respectively, inclusive of a 5 storey commercial hotel, retail, office and residential 
amenity podium. A total of 1,828 market strata residential units, 171 hotel suites, 
and approximately 297.21 m2 of retail space, 986.35 m2 of restaurant space, and 
906.84 m2 of office space are proposed. The proposed amendments compared 
with the prior proposal advanced through REZ #24-23 include:

Tower 1B; 
• the addition of a 171-suite hotel within four levels of the commercial 
podium;

As of November 30, 2023 the Housing Statutes (Residential Development) Amendment 
Act, 2023 amended Section 464 of the Local Government Act to prohibit local 
governments from holding a Public Hearing for rezoning applications that are all or 
predominantly (greater than 50%) residential, where the proposed rezoning is consistent 
with the City’s Official Community Plan. As such, a Public Hearing is prohibited from being 
held for this rezoning application.

forward the Rezoning Bylaw for First Reading, in accordance with section 467 of the 
Local Government Act, the City’s Public Notice Bylaw and Council policy. City staff will 
mail a notice to those properties that are within a 30 m radius of the subject site. A 
public notice will also be published on the City’s website, distributed as part of the City’s 
online newsletter, and a sign regarding the proposal will be posted on the site.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
A rezoning application has been received to permit the construction of a 12-storey 
mixed-use multiple-family development with a double-height commercial storey at street 
level. The development is comprised of 290 residential units, ground-oriented 
commercial uses fronting Kingsway, and underground parking. The proposal is 
consistent with the applicable Official Community Plan and the Edmonds Community 
Plan designations for the site. The purpose of this report is to provide Council with 
information on the proposal and to recommend that the Rezoning Bylaw be brought 
forward for First and Second Reading at a future date of Council.  
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
The proposed rezoning application is consistent with the following policies and plans 
adopted by Council: 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022), 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025), 
• Edmonds Community Plan (2025), 
• Economic Development Strategy (2007), 
• Social Sustainability Strategy (2011),  
• Environmental Sustainability Strategy (2016),  
• Transportation Plan (2021),  
• HOME: Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021), and 
• Burnaby District Energy Policy (2024). 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
2.1 The subject development site is located along Kingsway, between Colborne 
Avenue and Arcola Street, and is designated with base and overlay land use 
designations of Neighbourhood Commercial and Mid-Rise Apartment 1 
respectively (see Attachment 1). The Official Community Plan (OCP) requires that 
the base designation of Neighbourhood Commercial be present on the site as part 
of the development proposal. The site is within the Northwest Edmonds 
neighbourhood and is comprised of a single lot which is currently improved with a 
fast-food restaurant and drive-through.  
2.2 The applicant has now submitted a plan of development in alignment with the CM1 
Neighbourhood Commercial and R5 Mid-Rise Apartment 1 District guidelines, that 
is suitable for Council’s consideration of First and Second Reading of the 
Rezoning Bylaw. 
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1 The development proposal, as shown in Figure 1, is for a 12-storey mixed-use 
building with 290 residential dwelling units above commercial uses at street level 
fronting Kingsway. Vehicular access to underground parking is provided via the 
rear lane from Balmoral Street. The proposal includes indoor and outdoor rooftop 
amenity space. A more detailed summary of the proposed development, including

It is noted that the proposed development includes a rooftop amenity space, and a

single level rooftop amenity areas that do not exceed 25% of the total roof area 
are not considered a storey for the purposes of determining building height. 
Additionally, rooftop access, mechanical penthouses, and elevator overruns are 
excluded from the height calculation, and one of two storeys containing communal 
uses (such as bike parking) are excluded from the height calculation where the 
total floor to ceiling of the two storeys combined does not exceed the floor-to-
ceiling height of an adjacent commercial unit. The proposal meets these 
conditions and is therefore considered a 12-storey building in accordance with the 
Zoning Bylaw.

total of approximately 951 m2 is proposed along the Kingsway frontage, as shown 
in Figure 1. The inclusion of this commercial space satisfies the OCP requirement 
to provide a base neighbourhood commercial land use on the site. Since this is a 
required storey, it does not qualify as an eligible height increase for voluntary 
commercial, and the commercial storey is included in the permitted 12-storey 
base height provisions. The provision of commercial uses at this location is 
aligned with the Edmonds Community Plan which calls for the continuation of 
ground-level commercial uses along both sides of Kingsway in medium-density 
mixed-use building forms.

governments from holding a Public Hearing for rezoning applications that are all or 
predominantly (greater than 50%) residential, where the proposed rezoning is

prohibited from being held for this rezoning application.

notice will also be published on the City’s website, distributed as part of the City’s online

SUBJECT: 
BURNABY POVERTY REDUCTION STRATEGY 
 
PURPOSE: 
To seek Council endorsement of the Burnaby Poverty Reduction 
Strategy. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy, provided as Attachment 1 to the 
report titled “Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy,” dated March 11, 2026, be 
endorsed;  
THAT staff be authorized to work with community partners to undertake the 
development of an implementation plan; and 
THAT the Mayor write a letter to the Minister of Social Development and Poverty 
Reduction advocating to re-establish an ongoing funding program in partnership 
with the Union of BC Municipalities (UBCM) to support communities in 
implementing local poverty reduction initiatives and forward a copy of this report 
and attachments. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
The City was awarded a $25,000 grant from the Union of BC Municipalities to develop a 
community-based poverty reduction strategy in Burnaby. The project was developed in 
partnership with co-leads, the Burnaby Neighbourhood House and Burnaby Family Life. 
This report presents the finalized Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy along with a 
What We Heard Report summarizing the community engagement feedback and the 
Burnaby Inter-Agency Council resolution committing to adopting and implementing this 
work with community partners and interest holders in Burnaby. For next steps, this 
report seeks Council endorsement and further direction for staff to work with community 
partners to develop an implementation plan and advance actions in the Strategy.  
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
Reducing poverty at a local level is aligned with and supported by the following Council-
adopted policies, plans and strategies: 
• Economic Development Strategy (2007); 
• Social Sustainability Strategy (2011); 
• Equity Policy (1994, updated in 2020); 

Subject: Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy 
SPC Meeting March 11, 2026 ....................................................... Page 2 
 
• HOME: Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021); 
• Child Care Action Plan (2021); 
• Burnaby Transportation Plan (2021); 
• Burnaby Anti-Racism Framework (2024); 
• Food Systems Strategy (2024); 
• Accessibility Plan (2025); 
• Community Safety and Well-Being Plan (2025); and 
• Draft Official Community Plan (2025). 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
On February 6, 2023, Council authorized staff to apply to the Union of BC Municipalities 
(UBCM) Poverty Reduction Planning & Action Program for the maximum eligible 
amount of $25,000 in order to fund the development of a community-based Poverty 
Reduction Strategy. The Strategy proposed to gain a better understanding of the 
impacts of poverty in Burnaby and develop actions to alleviate it. On June 30, 2023, the 
UBCM awarded the City in the amount of $25,000 for the Strategy, along with the City 
also contributing $25,000 for this project.  
This report presents to Council the finalized Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy 
(Attachment 1), along with a What We Heard Report summarizing the engagement 
feedback received during the drafting of the Strategy (Attachment 2). For next steps, 
this report seeks Council endorsement and further direction for staff to develop an 
implementation plan with community partners to advance the actions in the Strategy. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1 Strategy Process 
In January 2024, the City issued a call for proposal for a community agency to lead the 
development of a community-based poverty reduction strategy in Burnaby. From this 
process, the Burnaby Neighbourhood House and Burnaby Family Life were selected as 
co-leads on the project, with City staff providing administrative and in-kind support to the 
project.  
With the support of a consultant, a Poverty Reduction Steering Committee (Committee) 
was formed to provide overall guidance on the development of the Strategy, co-design 
the community engagement process, and provide feedback on deliverables and reports. 
The Committee reflected diverse sectors that provide various programs and services to 
the Burnaby community, as well as members with lived and living experience of poverty. 
The Committee, co-chaired by Burnaby Neighbourhood House and Burnaby Family 
Life, met six times throughout the project. The work program was divided into three 
phases: 
• Phase 1 – Understanding and Learning (May – August 2024) 
• Phase 2 – Engaging and Drafting (September 2024 – April 2025) 
• Phase 3 – Final Strategy (May – July 2025) 

Subject: Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy 
SPC Meeting March 11, 2026 ....................................................... Page 3 
 
Phase 1 included planning the project process, launching and meeting with the 
Committee, conducting a literature review, reviewing Burnaby’s poverty datasets and 
designing the community engagement strategy and activities. 
Phase 2 had two sub-phases of engagement: 
Phase 2a included holding Committee meetings and conducting community 
engagement activities, which included a city-wide survey, listening circles, 
interviews, and a partners’ workshop with service providers and people with 
lived/living experience of poverty. 
Phase 2b included analyzing the engagement data, hosting a partners’ forum to 
discuss results and obtain further feedback, preparing a “What We Heard” 
community engagement report and drafting the Strategy. 
Phase 3 included finalizing the Strategy and identifying opportunities for 
implementation, where possible.  
The project is now complete and staff are seeking Council endorsement of the Strategy. 
3.2 Community Engagement Overview 
The What We Heard Report (Attachment 2) details the community engagement for the 
strategy. The community engagement activities emphasized hearing from people with 
lived/living experience of poverty while service providers shared their expertise. The 
activities were designed to engage people with diverse and intersecting factors. The 
project team, steering committee members and other community organizations 
collectively designed and supported the engagement through staff time, 
communications and by providing a meeting space. Some organizations also provided 
language, translation and/or technology support for participants. 
A summary of the engagement activities and participation are as follows:

Some of the key takeaways from the engagement include:  
• overall frustration and hopelessness about participants’ ability to meet their daily 
needs (e.g. high and increasing costs for food, housing and other daily 
essentials); 

Subject: Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy 
SPC Meeting March 11, 2026 ....................................................... Page 4 
 
• health issues/events, language and ageism were the top three social barriers to 
financial stability identified by survey participants; 
• a sense of shame and loss of dignity was identified by Listening Circles 
participants; 
• lack of awareness and understanding of existing support and services available 
in Burnaby was noted by participants (and how to better access them) as well as 
capacity challenges to meet the demand and complexity of needs was cited by 
service providers;  
• employment barriers due to health conditions, disability, language, age, 
immigration status and the need for Canadian-based work experience was noted; 
and 
• trauma due to family violence, racism, experience of war and other factors impact 
participants’ sense of agency to build financial stability.   
Participants also noted that the What We Heard Report provides a point-in-time 
snapshot on the current state of poverty but ongoing community engagement and 
resources to continue understanding the longer-term impacts of reducing poverty in 
Burnaby is needed. 
3.3 Strategy Overview 
The Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy (Attachment 1) details the work completed 
over the past year and provides recommended actions to advance poverty reduction 
initiatives over the long term. The success of the Strategy will require all levels of 
government and community partners to work together on the actions identified to 
address poverty at a local level.  
Poverty reduction actions are organized into domains, referred to as “Game Changers,” 
based upon their ability to impact both individual and collective poverty. For Burnaby, 
the Game Changers identified through the engagement process and detailed further in 
the recommended actions, in alphabetical order, are:

Under each Game Changer, the recommended actions are further organized by the 
type of approach each action would be addressed by. These include: 
• Program: opportunities to scale existing programs or develop new programs to 
address needs; 
• Partnership: opportunities for increased partner coordination or coalitions to 
collaborate on programs and/or advocacy; and 
• Advocacy: opportunities to advance or promote systemic change by all levels of 
government. 
3.4 Implementation and Next Steps 
Some Game Changers intersect with other Council-approved plans, strategies and 
programs. To support implementation of the actions outlined in the Strategy, an 
implementation plan that identifies alignment, roles and responsibilities, timelines and 
resources is needed. As such, staff seek Council endorsement of the Strategy, in 
addition to direction for staff to develop an implementation plan with community 
partners, understanding that this project is intended to be community-based. 
Endorsement of the Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy will allow the City and 
community partners to pursue future funding opportunities. In the development of an 
implementation plan, staff would work with community partners on organizing potential 
workshops and sessions. If there are further actions or requests from the City, staff 
would report back on any staffing and/or financial implications of such work at a future 
meeting. 
3.5  Lower Mainland Local Government Association (LMLGA) and the Union of BC 
Municipalities (UBCM)  
In 2024, the Provincial Government updated the Poverty Reduction Strategy Act, 
introducing more ambitious poverty reduction targets than those set in 2018. For 
reference, the updated Poverty Reduction Strategy Act aims to reduce the poverty rate 
in 2034 below the poverty rate in 2016 by at least: 
• 60% among all persons (up from 25%); 

Subject: Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy 
SPC Meeting March 11, 2026 ....................................................... Page 6 
 
• 75% among persons under 18 years of age (up from 50%); and 
• 50% among persons 65 years of age and older (new target). 
UBCM had initially awarded the City to undertake this work with the previous poverty 
rates set in 2018. Since this project is aimed to align and support the provincial poverty 
reduction goals, the Strategy reflects the latest poverty reduction targets set in 2024. 
However, implementing local poverty reduction initiatives while attempting to meet the 
updated provincial targets require support from other levels of government and 
community partners. 
On February 11, 2025, Council approved three resolutions to submit to the LMLGA 
Annual General Meeting and the UBCM Convention. One of the resolutions was to 
respond to the funding shortfalls to implement local poverty reduction initiatives, as this 
was an action already identified as part of developing the Strategy:   
Resolution: Continue Funding Programs for Local Poverty Reduction Initiatives  
WHEREAS the cost of basic goods and services continues to increase for people 
living in poverty and other low-income British Columbians; and 
WHEREAS the Provincial Government is encouraging local governments and 
community partners to support the updated targets set to reduce the overall 
poverty rate, child poverty rate and seniors’ poverty rate without adequate 
resources;  
THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT the UBCM request the Provincial 
Government to re-establish a grant program in partnership with the UBCM to 
support communities in implementing local poverty reduction initiatives. 
At the LMLGA Annual Conference, the City’s resolution was endorsed with a minor 
amendment that now seeks the provincial government to establish an ongoing funding 
program, as opposed to a grant program.1 The UBCM Resolutions Book2 identifies that 
the resolution is currently endorsed by the UBCM Resolutions Committee. The UBCM 
Annual Convention took place on September 24-26, 2025 and the resolution was 
endorsed as stated in the Resolutions Disposition3. Staff will monitor the next step, 
which is for the Provincial Government to respond back to each endorsed resolution.   
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Should Council endorse the Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy, the project page of 
the City website would be updated to make the Strategy and What We Heard Report

available. These documents will also be made available on the websites of community

On December 4, 2025, the Burnaby Inter-Agency Council (BIAC) hosted their Annual

TO: 
FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE (FMC) 
 
FROM: 
DEPUTY CHIEF ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER AND CHIEF FINANCIAL 
OFFICER  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
CITY INVESTMENTS – 2025 YEAR END REPORT 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide an update on the 2025 Investment Program and present a 
forecast for 2026. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “City Investments – 2025 Year End Report”, dated March 
17, 2026, be received for information.  
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
Part 6, Division 3, Section 183 of the Community Charter states that the City may invest 
or reinvest money that is not immediately required for expenditures. Council has assigned 
the responsibility for the management of the investment portfolio to the Chief Financial 
Officer (Amendment Bylaw No. 14408).  
2.0 BACKGROUND 
This report highlights events that have occurred in the investment program during 2025 
and the forecast for 2026.  The City of Burnaby’s Investment Portfolio on December 31, 
2025 totaled $2.08 billion (2024 – $2.21 billion). 
Diversification and a prescribed investment strategy have provided the City with a 2025 
return of $106.63 million at an annual yield of 4.43% (2024 – $107.91 million at 4.36%).  
The income and yield earned in 2025 was higher than expected as the City was able to 
extend investment holding terms prior to capital project payments and the Municipal 
Finance Authority of British Columbia (MFA) Diversified Multi-Asset Class Fund (DMAC) 
provided another solid year of interest, dividends and capital gains income.  A portion of 
these earnings - $9.13 million - offset operating costs in 2025.  For 2026, Treasury 
Services will continue to monitor markets for investment opportunities across the yield 
curve, taking into consideration liquidity constraints while ensuring capital preservation, 
yield management and long-term growth for the City. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
The City’s Investment Policy expands upon the legislative requirements of the Community 
Charter.  Annual review of the City’s policy provides assurances that the City is abiding 
by a professional set of standards for the management of public funds and confirmation 
that the City is maintaining a policy framework that includes prudence, diversification, 
strong internal controls, delegation of authority, and reporting transparency.   
 
 

Subject: City Investments – 2025 Year End Report 
Open FMC Meeting March 17, 2026 Page 2 
 
The City invests in both short and long-term investment products to ensure adequate 
cash flow liquidity and as much as possible, long-term sustainable growth. The 
investment portfolio is directly impacted by changes in both short and long-term interest 
rates; and therefore, maintains longer term investment holdings in select Municipal 
Finance Authority of British Columbia (MFA) pooled funds as a diversification strategy. 
Investment holdings may consist of debt issued by the Federal Government of Canada, 
and approved Provinces, Canadian Banks and Credit Unions as identified in Attachment 
1. The Community Charter also allows the City to invest in any of the pooled investment 
funds and High Interest Savings Accounts offered by the MFA.  Pooled funds offered by 
the MFA can include corporate debt, which typically provides for increased yields. The 
Community Charter restricts the City from investing in corporate debt and other security 
types unless we invest directly into the MFA’s funds.  
The Bank of Canada conducts monetary policy through adjustments to the Target for the 
Overnight Rate, which affects deposits, loans, and other interest rates. The Bank Prime 
Rate on December 31, 2025, was 4.45%, which is 100 basis points lower than one year 
ago.  Table 1 provides 2025 average interest rates and comparable returns.

In 2025, the City maintained our initial $10 million investment in the MFA Fossil Fuel Free 
Short-term Bond Fund, $75 million in the MFA Mortgage Fund and $400 million in the 
MFA DMAC. These funds had varied performance in 2025 with the DMAC Fund leading 
the way with an annualized return of 13.60% based on a mark to market measurement 
(2024 – 17.48%).  The Mortgage Fund had an annual yield of 4.75% (2024 - 6.13%) and 
the Fossil Fuel Free Short-term Bond Fund yielded 3.89% (2024 - 5.39%).  The 
investment positions are based on the funds targeted timeframe to protect from short-
term market volatility and extreme interest rate changes.  

Subject: City Investments – 2025 Year End Report 
Open FMC Meeting March 17, 2026 Page 3 
 
3.1 Investment Limits and Banking Relationships 
Treasury Services monitors the financial institutions we invest in as part of our ongoing 
risk mitigation strategy and investment procedures. The City’s investment banking 
relationships remained consistent during 2025. For short-term money market 
investments, the City of Burnaby invested with British Columbia, Alberta, 
Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Federal Credit Unions, as well as High Interest Savings 
accounts at the Bank of Montreal, Canadian Imperial Bank Canada, Bank of Nova 
Scotia, National Bank of Canada and Royal Bank of Canada.   
During the year, as tax revenues are received, the Chief Financial Officer may approve 
adjustments to the limits or the addition of new guarantors to meet portfolio needs. 
The 2026 limits are identified in Attachment 1 and include corresponding Dominion 
Bond ratings where applicable.   
3.2 Socially Responsible Investing 
The City’s Investment Portfolio and Policy direction will continue to gradually 
implement strategies that support the City of Burnaby’s Corporate Strategic Plan, 
Environmental Sustainability Strategy and Community Energy and Emissions 
Plan.  The City’s recognition of SRI and ESG strategies will continue to evolve as 
Canadian and global standards evolve, while simultaneously investing prudently to 
ensure long-term financial sustainability.  The continued challenge is the disparity and 
lack of standardization within the industry and loosely based interpretations.  
Governance and a strong audit structure globally will be required to advance further 
in this area.   
3.3 Outlook for 2026 
Canada and the world faced many disruptors in 2025.  An inequity - driven by 
isolationist actions and ideological pressures – has been a negative catalyst onto a 
world predicated by trade globalization. The likes for which has been an unravelling 
of trade guarantees and national security; a cycle that may have only just begun.  It is 
not anticipated the Bank of Canada will reduce interest rates further in 2026, and 
despite many negative headlines, the central bank may be limited to actions as the 
risk of stagflation grows.   
Canada specifically will be managing several problem areas in 2026 and beyond such 
as slow economic growth and trade volatility, housing affordability and construction 
slowdown, demographic aging and labour force pressure, geopolitical and national 
security tensions, supply chain and trade uncertainty, and mounting federal and 
provincial debt. 
The City’s focus on preservation and liquidity in 2025 will continue throughout 2026. 
This means a significant decrease in active longer-term investments and yield curve 
positioning – which in 2025 represented the lowest volume of such activities in over 
two decades. This restriction is based on operational and capital program needs.   
 
 

Subject: City Investments – 2025 Year End Report 
Open FMC Meeting March 17, 2026 Page 4 
 
For the City of Burnaby in 2026, we will maintain our buy and hold strategy with the 
MFA Pooled Funds, which will provide interest income, dividends and capital gains 
through important market diversification.  As maturities occur within the portfolio and 
are reinvested, we will continue to shorten our term duration and will be subject to the 
lower rates as seen by the already implemented cuts by the Bank of Canada over the 
last 18 months. 
The City is projecting an annual yield of 3.20% for 2026, translating to $71 million in 
investment interest income which represents a significant decrease from the 2025 
returns.  The portion from investment earnings to be contributed to operating costs for 
2026 is budgeted at $8.3 million (2025 - $9.13 million actual). This projection will also 
be affected by the returns generated by all direct investments and the MFA pooled 
funds and any fluctuation in the portfolio balance due to operating and capital 
expenditures and deposits - the timing of which is fluid. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Not applicable 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Investment results can vary from the projected figures based on the City’s capital budget 
activities and revenues.  Projections are based on expected budget spend on civic 
projects and our ability to improve earnings when considering liquidity needs as projects 
and activity timelines are modified and costs are adjusted for inflation and other impacts.  
Respectfully submitted, 
Doug Spindler, Director Treasury Services 
For  
Noreen Kassam, Deputy Chief Administrative Officer and Chief Financial Officer

Klimek, Deputy General Manager Finance and Ratan Grewal, Deputy General Manager Finance.

SUBJECT:

PURPOSE: 
To provide a status update (SU) on significant civic projects. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “SU: Significant Civic Projects – March 2026” dated March 
17, 2026, be received for information. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This report provides a status update (SU) on significant civic projects managed by the 
Lands and Facilities Department (Civic Projects). 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
Advancement of these projects is pursuant to the following major civic policies and 
strategies: 
 
• Official Community Plan (1998), 
• Social Sustainability Strategy (2011), 
• Environmental Sustainability Strategy (2016), 
• Burnaby Equity Policy (1994, updated 2020), 
• Burnaby Climate Action Framework (2020), and the 
• Burnaby Strategic Plan (2022). 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The City of Burnaby has experienced significant growth and changing needs in recent 
years and is projected to grow by 100,000 new residents by 2050. In order to serve both 
existing and future residents, the City has identified the need for a number of significant 
civic projects in order to support its goals of creating vibrant communities and a city that 
is sustainable, inclusive, and livable. 
 
Civic projects typically progress through three primary development phases: planning, 
design, and construction, with some phases occurring concurrently depending on the 
project delivery method. Upon completion of the project, the new facility is handed over 
to the user department to establish operations of the programs and/or services. 
 
 

Subject: SU: Significant Civic Projects – March 2026 
Open FMC Meeting March 17, 2026 …………Page 2 
 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
A status update for the significant civic projects referenced in this report is provided 
below. Figure 1 is a visual overview of project progress referencing the primary 
development phases referenced in Section 2.0 above. Figure 2 provides an update on 
the spending against the overall budget for each project. 
 
 
Figure 1 – Project Schedules 
 
 
Figure 2 – Project Financials 
 
3.1 Mintara Restaurant Expansion 
The patio area at Mintara Restaurant atop Burnaby Mountain is being expanded into 
a 2,000 square-foot enclosed, all-weather patio. The renovation features glass 
windows, radiant floor heating and air conditioning, and a full bar and barbecue 
section. 
 
Earthworks for the new foundations have now been completed. Current and 
upcoming construction activities include foundation formwork, underground utility 
upgrades, and installation of foundation drainage. The project remains on schedule 
and on budget. Project handover to the Parks, Recreation and Culture Department 
(PRC) is currently anticipated for Q3 2026. 
 
3.2 James Cowan Theatre Renewal 
As the new Shadbolt main stage, the James Cowan Theatre will be Burnaby’s 
primary professional multi-use presentation space. The new facility will cater to a 
variety of arts programming, including theatre, visual arts, literary and media arts, 

Subject: SU: Significant Civic Projects – March 2026 
Open FMC Meeting March 17, 2026 …………Page 3 
 
and events. Construction is progressing with exterior windows, roofing, drywall, and 
elevator installation work underway. Construction impacts continue to be mitigated 
as much as possible through measures such as in-place parking restrictions and 
maintaining pedestrian access. Project handover to PRC is currently anticipated for 
Q1 2027. The project remains on schedule and on budget. 
 
3.3 Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre 
The Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre will be one of the largest aquatic facilities in 
BC and a regional hub for recreation and sport, featuring a ten-lane 50-metre pool, a 
combined leisure and six-lane 25-metre lap-pool, and an NHL-sized arena. A range 
of community spaces are also incorporated in the design, including multipurpose 
rooms and the Burnaby Sports Hall of Fame. Construction milestones include the 
structural steel erection of the administrative lobby area that has recently been 
completed and waterproofing of the 50-metre pool basin that is currently in progress. 
 
Preliminary design concepts are underway for a potential pedestrian overpass in 
order to evaluate options, feasibility, and cost recovery mechanisms. The technical 
analysis and recommendations will be conducted in the coming weeks. 
 
Construction works requiring the crawler crane have now been completed and the 
crawler crane has been dismantled and transported offsite. The watermain 
replacement work has been substantially completed. Roadworks on Joe Sakic Way 
and Sperling Avenue are underway for new sidewalks and wider pavement with 
additional on-street parking, and construction will continue with some traffic impacts 
until the fall of this year. Public parking and pedestrian access continue to be 
maintained. Project handover to PRC is currently anticipated for Q1 2028 with the 
project currently trending ahead of schedule. The project remains on budget. 
 
3.4 Community Safety Building 
A new three-story, 129,000-square-foot RCMP detachment is being constructed at 
the corner of Ledger Avenue and Norland Avenue to replace the existing 
detachment. Construction activities include roof deck installation and concrete 
pouring for the parking garage. Envelope and building façade tenders are underway. 
 
Design of the project is well underway and progressing in accordance with target 
value design principles to maximize value and proactive design solutions. The 
design processes have been completed for the interior walls, ceilings, and exterior 
design of the building envelope. Design is currently underway for the interior 
requirements and site/landscaping plans, with design completion anticipated by Q1 
2027. 
 
Project completion is anticipated for the end of Q4 2027, with the RCMP 
commissioning period anticipated to be approximately six months. The project 
remains on schedule and on budget. 

Subject: SU: Significant Civic Projects – March 2026 
Open FMC Meeting March 17, 2026 …………Page 4 
 
3.5 Cameron Community Centre and Library 
The new Cameron Community Centre and Library will be the largest community 
centre in Burnaby, featuring an aquatic facility, gymnasium, weight room, expanded 
library, and civic plaza. Construction continues with pool and gym foundation wall 
suspended slab concrete installation underway. Project handover to PRC is currently 
anticipated for Q2 2028. The project remains on budget. A project highlights 
presentation is scheduled for the Committee at its March 2026 meeting. 
 
3.6 Brentwood Community Centre 
The Brentwood Community Centre will be a multi-level facility within the Brentwood 
Block development site, featuring community gathering spaces, a fitness centre, a 
gymnasium, a podium-level outdoor park, music and art rooms, and a public plaza. 
The project schedule aligns with the Grosvenor Brentwood Master Plan 
development timelines and development of the operating agreement is underway. 
Construction on the development site is currently underway, with construction of the 
community centre portions of the building currently anticipated to start in Q3 2027 
and be completed in Q1 2030. The project remains on budget. 
3.7 Community Facilities Fit-Out Project 
The Community Facilities Fit-Out Project will provide the required furniture, fixtures, 
and equipment for the James Cowan Theatre, the Community Safety Building, 
Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre, Cameron Community Centre and Library, and the 
Brentwood Community Centre. This project will provide a streamlined approach to 
support the fitting out of these major capital projects and to maximize efficiencies 
through economies of scale. Delivery and installation of the required furniture, 
fixtures, and equipment will be coordinated with each individual project timeline to 
ensure all fit-out requirements will be in place for the opening of each facility. The 
coordination work as part of this project spans both capital and operating needs and 
the required expenditures will need to be funded accordingly through the annual 
budget process. The initial procurement processes are currently underway, with 
coordination work continuing until the completion of the last major project in 2030. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Communication plans are in place for each project, with ongoing engagement with key 
users and staff groups. External communication to the wider public regarding any 
impacts occurs through dedicated project webpages, public notices via the City’s 
communication channels, and continued outreach by City staff. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS 
Funding for the noted significant civic building projects is included in the 2026-2030 
Capital Plan. Budget requirements for operations will be brought forward by the Parks, 
Recreation and Culture Department for Council’s consideration as the subject projects 
advance. 
 
 

Subject: SU: Significant Civic Projects – March 2026 
Open FMC Meeting March 17, 2026 …………Page 5 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
James Lota, General Manager Lands and Facilities

GENERAL MANAGER PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
OCP: BURNABY 2050 IMPLEMENTATION ACTION PLAN 
PROJECT INITIATION 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide Council with an overview of the Official Community Plan 
(OCP) Implementation Action Plan. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “OCP: Burnaby 2050 Implementation Action Plan Project 
Initiation,” dated March 4, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
An OCP broadly connects with all policies established in a community. The OCP has 
been considered in conjunction with key City plans, policies and bylaws, including the 
following. 
• Zoning Bylaw (1965) and Zoning Bylaw Rewrite project (2025 and ongoing);  
• Economic Development Strategy (2007); 
• Social Sustainability Strategy (2011); 
• Environmental Sustainability Strategy (2016); 
• Climate Action Framework (2020); 
• Tenant Assistance Policy (2020) 
• HOME: Burnaby’s Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021); 
• Connecting Burnaby: Burnaby’s Transportation Plan (2021); 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022); 
• Metro 2050 - Regional Growth Strategy (2022); 
• Burnaby Interim Housing Needs Report (2024); 
• Transit Oriented Area Bylaw (2024); 
• Housing Needs Report (2024); and 
• Urban Forest Strategy (2025). 
 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
 
At its regular meeting on December 9, 2025, Council adopted Bylaw No. 14762 
‘Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan’ (OCP).  Burnaby 2050, the new OCP for the 
City, was the culmination of a multi-year process that began on February 14, 2022, to 
initiate its development to replace the 1998 OCP Bylaw. 

Subject: OCP: Burnaby 2025 Implementation Action Plan Project Initiation 
PDC Meeting March 4, 2026  ......................................................................................... Page 2 
 
A subsequent Council report on June 20, 2022, officially launched the Burnaby 2050 
Planning Our City Together project as a multi-year, multi-phase process to create a new 
long-term vision for the community to 2050 (Figure 1).  
Figure 1: Phases of Burnaby 2050 Planning Our City Together Project 
Each step in the Burnaby 2050 project included engagement and consultation.  Over 
the four-year process, City staff spoke with almost 7,000 community members, received 
over 23,000 written comments and met with 63 different community partner 
organizations.  
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
Part D of the Burnaby 2050 OCP lays out a plan for its implementation.  Key 
implementation mechanisms rely on a suite of statutory land use tools, including the 
Zoning Bylaw, Development Permit Areas and guidelines, and Community Plans, as 
well as the creation of a dedicated Burnaby 2050 Implementation Action Plan (IAP).    
Providing a clear plan for implementation and monitoring progress commits the City to 
be accountable to the community members, partners and organizations who worked to 
shape the OCP’s vision, goals, and policy actions.    
This report provides a high-level summary of the proposed contents of the IAP. 
3.1  OCP Implementation Action Plan 
The IAP will lay out a framework for implementation of the OCP’s policy actions, as well 
as monitoring and reporting on progress towards meeting its goals and objectives.  It is 
intended to encourage continued engagement and collaboration across City 
departments to implement the policy actions in Burnaby 2050.  
Regular reporting on progress of implementation and resulting outcomes will 
demonstrate the accountability and the City’s commitment to keeping the community’s 
vision - as articulated in the OCP - alive and relevant.  The IAP will be designed around 
the principle of adaptive management, where policies and practices are continually 

Subject: OCP: Burnaby 2025 Implementation Action Plan Project Initiation 
PDC Meeting March 4, 2026  ......................................................................................... Page 3 
 
improved based on learning from the outcomes of monitoring and evaluation. Through 
the Adaptive Management Framework (Figure 2) the City will be able to evaluate 
outcomes and make adjustments to priorities or even amendments to the OCP, to better 
meet the OCP’s goals and objectives.  
Figure 2: Adaptive Management Framework 
 
The IAP will include three sections: a Monitoring Framework, an Implementation 
Strategy and a Reporting Plan (Figure 3). 
Figure 3: Implementation Action Plan Structure 
Implementation Action Plan
Monitoring Framework
Implementation Strategy
Reporting Plan

organized by OCP Objective

assignment of OCP Policy

timelines
Adaptive Management Framework
 
• Monitoring Framework: 
The Monitoring Framework will include a series of Key Performance 
Indicators (KPIs) that demonstrate progress towards each Burnaby 2050 
objective. Indicators will be selected based on their relevance to each specific 
objective, as well as data availability. The Monitoring Framework will specify 
the data source, baseline year, what is being measured and why, the 
frequency of reporting, the geography at which the data is available, and the 

Subject: OCP: Burnaby 2025 Implementation Action Plan Project Initiation 
PDC Meeting March 4, 2026  ......................................................................................... Page 4 
 
directionality that supports the goal and objective.  There may also be 
references to historical data to measure past trends. 
 
• Implementation Strategy: 
The Implementation Strategy will outline the priority, assignment, and status 
of OCP policy action implementation.  It will specify which team(s) are 
responsible for the implementation of each policy action, whether those 
actions are ongoing or in current work programs, and lay out general 
timelines to guide the completion of the remaining policy actions.  The 
Implementation Strategy will also provide high level consideration of 
resources needed to implement particular policy actions. 
 
• Reporting Plan: 
The Reporting Plan will describe how and when the OCP team will report on 
implementation progress.  It will include a report template and describe the 
design of an online monitoring dashboard.  The Reporting Plan will also 
describe a reporting mechanism for the status of the implementation of 
Burnaby 2050 policy actions including their level of completion or any 
additional policy actions added to City work programs. The Reporting Plan will 
also include details on how adaptive management will be used to evaluate 
progress towards the OCP’s goals and objectives and an approach to amend 
plans, policies and operations where necessary to achieve success.  
 
3.2  IAP Project Timeline and Resourcing 
Early background work on the Burnaby 2050 IAP began in late 2025. In Q1 and Q2 
2026, staff will focus on preparing the Monitoring Framework and the online dashboard. 
In Q2, work will begin on the Implementation Strategy. Drafting the Reporting Plan and 
overarching IAP will take place in Q4 2026. The final Burnaby 2050 IAP is anticipated to 
be ready for PDC and Council in Q1 2027.  
Development of the Burnaby 2050 IAP will be done by staff. No external consulting 
support is anticipated. 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Engagement on Burnaby 2050 took place in five phases between 2022 and 2025 and 
included opportunities for the public to provide input on the implementation of Burnaby 
2050. Development of the Burnaby 2050 IAP will primarily involve engagement within 
Burnaby’s departments, although some regional agencies, such as TransLink, may also 
be engaged. Once adopted by Council, the IAP will be posted on the Burnaby 2050 
webpage.  
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Sufficient funding for all OCP-related work in 2026 is included within the Planning and 
Development Department operating budget in the 2026-2030 Financial Plan.

briefs.title: Burnaby supports Solo Karaoke liquor licence at Kingsway location.
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council supported a liquor primary licence for Solo Karaoke at 102–6462 Kingsway. This follows an investigation into previous allegations. Council will send a recommendation to the Liquor and Cannabis Regulation Branch.

briefs.title: Burnaby Confirms 2026 Sewer Parcel Tax Roll.
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council confirmed the 2026 Sewer Parcel Tax Roll. This roll lists all properties that will be charged a tax for sewer services. The Parcel Tax Roll Review Panel confirmed and authenticated the tax roll.

briefs.title: Burnaby Approves Housing Authority's 2026-2030 Strategic Plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation’s (BHA) 2026-2030 Strategic Plan. The plan guides the BHA's work in developing both non-market and market rental housing in Burnaby. The plan aims for 1,000 housing unit starts by 2030.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Burnaby Approved UBCM Membership Dues Payment for 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved paying $36,250.57 to the Union of BC Municipalities (UBCM) for its 2026 membership. The UBCM gives local governments a united voice, letting them share experiences and create common positions to present to other levels of government.

briefs.title: Burnaby to Buy Water Metering Tech for City-Wide Universal Metering
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved a $4.2 million contract with Neptune Technology Group Canada Co. for water metering technology. This is the first phase of the Universal Water Metering Strategy. The city will install about 5,500 water meters for meter-ready properties and 500 meters in homes with secondary suites.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Burnaby Approves Modular Four-Plex Housing Unit Development on 14th Avenue
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a contract for $2,626,050 to Echelon Creative Inc. to develop a modular four-plex housing unit at 8315 14th Avenue. This pilot project aims to test if factory-built housing is a good option for small multi-unit buildings in low-density areas.

briefs.title: Burnaby Approves $29.8 Million in Hired Equipment Service Contracts
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved awarding 19 contracts for hired equipment services, totaling an estimated $29.8 million over three years. These contracts allow the city to supplement its resources with specialized labor and equipment for construction, maintenance, and emergency work.

briefs.title: Burnaby Approved $1.15 Million Asphalt Paving Contract Renewal with Blackrete
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a contract renewal with Blackrete Paving Ltd. for asphalt paving services. The contract is worth $1,155,000, including taxes, and covers the period from April 1, 2026, to March 31, 2027. Blackrete provides paving and concrete services as needed to support City projects.

briefs.title: Burnaby to work with developer on Carrigan Court mixed-use project.
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council authorized staff to work with an applicant on a revised plan for a high-density mixed-use project at 3856 and 3870 Carrigan Court. The plan includes about 1,133 strata units, 141 rental replacement units, and 300 inclusionary units.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Burnaby Approved Rezoning for Amended Mixed-Use Development on Grand Promenade
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved a rezoning bylaw for 3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858/9898 Gatineau Place. The rezoning allows for an amended mixed-use commercial/residential development, including restaurant and hotel uses, plus additional market strata residential units.
briefs.action_required: Review the updated development plans for potential impacts to your business.
briefs.industries: Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Burnaby Council OKs Rezoning for 12-Storey Mixed-Use Building on Kingsway
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved moving forward with a rezoning bylaw for 6535 Kingsway. The plan is for a 12-storey building with 290 apartments and ground-level commercial space. The building will connect to Burnaby's district energy utility. If the developer chooses to build strata housing (condos) instead of rentals, 5% of the units must be set aside as affordable rentals.
briefs.action_required: If building strata, prepare for inclusionary rental requirements.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Burnaby Approved Self-Storage Facility Rezoning at 2757 Norland Ave
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved moving forward with a rezoning bylaw for 2757 Norland Avenue. The rezoning allows for a five-story self-storage building. A public hearing will not be held because the rezoning aligns with Burnaby's Official Community Plan. The rezoning must be finalized by December 9, 2026.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Burnaby Council Endorsed Poverty Reduction Strategy
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council endorsed the Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy. The strategy aims to reduce poverty by addressing the cost of living, education, employment, family support, health, and housing. The city will work with community partners to create an implementation plan.
briefs.action_required: Review the Burnaby Poverty Reduction Strategy to identify potential opportunities.
briefs.industries: Retail, Food Service, Healthcare, Labour & Employment

briefs.title: Burnaby received building permit activity reports for January and February 2026.
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council received reports about building permits issued in January and February 2026. The reports give Council information about construction happening in Burnaby.

briefs.title: Burnaby Receives 2025 Investment Report, Forecasts $71 Million Income for 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council received a report on the City's 2025 investments. The City's investment portfolio totaled $2.08 billion in 2025, earning $106.63 million with a 4.43% yield. Council projects a $71 million investment income for 2026, with an expected yield of 3.20%.

briefs.title: Burnaby to expand Mintara Restaurant patio, renew James Cowan Theatre.
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council received an update on several significant civic projects. These include the Mintara Restaurant patio expansion, the James Cowan Theatre renewal, and the Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre, among others. The projects aim to support Burnaby's growth and create sustainable, inclusive, and livable communities.

briefs.title: Burnaby to Implement 2050 Official Community Plan Action Plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council received a report about the Burnaby 2050 Implementation Action Plan (IAP). The IAP will monitor and report on the city's progress towards meeting the goals outlined in the Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (OCP). The IAP includes a framework, implementation strategy, and reporting plan.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant
